tasks.title: Gather Complete Financial Picture
tasks.what: A systematic inventory of every asset, debt, income source, and financial obligation you and your spouse hold.
tasks.why: Courts divide assets based on a complete financial picture. Hidden assets discovered during divorce cost $15,000–$45,000 in legal fees to uncover. Missing even one account can shift the settlement by tens of thousands.
steps: List all bank accounts (joint, individual, business) with balances and property classification.
List all investment accounts, including cryptocurrency, with cost basis and current value.
Gather 3 years of tax returns (personal and business).
Document all real property: primary residence, vacation homes, rental properties with mortgage balances.
Document all retirement accounts: 401(k), IRA, pensions, stock options, RSUs with vesting schedules.
List all debts: mortgages, car loans, credit cards, student loans, medical debt.
Calculate net income for both spouses from all sources.
Document all insurance policies (life, health, disability, auto, home).

terms.def: In 9 US states, assets acquired during marriage are owned equally by both spouses.
In 41 states, assets are divided "fairly" but not necessarily 50/50.
Assets owned before marriage, gifts, or inheritances kept separate — belong to one spouse only.
Assets acquired during marriage with marital funds, regardless of whose name is on the account.

tasks.contacts: State courts for divorce checklists • CPA familiar with divorce • Certified Financial Divorce Specialist through IDFA
mistakes: Forgetting to value retirement accounts at current balance, not contribution amount.
Not accounting for tax implications (a $100K 401(k) is worth less than $100K in savings after taxes).
Hiding assets — courts impose sanctions and attorney fee penalties if discovered.
Assuming one spouse doesn't know about an account.

tasks.title: Protect Your Credit During Divorce
tasks.what: Steps to prevent your spouse from damaging your credit and to separate financial lives before the divorce is final.
tasks.why: A spouse can open credit cards or stop paying joint debts, dropping your score 100+ points in weeks. In community property states, you remain liable for 50% of debt created during marriage even after divorce.
steps: Place a fraud alert on your credit file with all three bureaus (Equifax, Experian, TransUnion).
Request free credit reports at annualcreditreport.com and check for unknown accounts.
Freeze your credit at all three bureaus — no new accounts without your PIN.
Change passwords on all financial accounts using a password manager.
Close joint credit cards once paid off; get written confirmation.
Remove your spouse as authorized user on your credit cards.
Separate utility accounts (gas, electric, water, internet, phone).
Screenshot every joint statement and save to cloud storage only you can access.

terms.def: A lock preventing new accounts from being opened in your name without your unique PIN.
Someone with permission to use an account but not responsible for paying it.
Debt both spouses agreed to; both remain liable even after divorce unless creditor releases you.

tasks.contacts: Equifax (1-800-685-1111) • Experian (1-888-397-3742) • TransUnion (1-800-680-7289) • FTC (ftc.gov) for credit protection guides
mistakes: Not freezing credit until after fraudulent accounts appear.
Assuming a settlement agreement releases you from joint debt — only the creditor can release you.
Closing accounts too early, lowering your credit score.
Not updating your address at the bank — mail can be intercepted.

tasks.title: Understand Retirement Account Division and QDRO
tasks.what: How 401(k)s, IRAs, pensions, and other retirement accounts are divided in divorce without tax penalties.
tasks.why: Retirement accounts are often the largest marital asset. Dividing them wrong triggers 20% federal withholding + 10% early withdrawal penalty. A QDRO allows penalty-free transfer; without it, the owner faces immediate taxes.
steps: Identify all retirement accounts in both names with balances and vesting schedules.
Determine how the account will be divided (typically 50/50 of the marital portion).
For 401(k)s and pensions: have your attorney draft a QDRO ($300–$800 per order).
Submit the QDRO to the plan administrator — review takes 30–90 days.
Receiving spouse rolls funds to their own IRA (no tax) or takes a distribution with 20% withholding.
For IRAs: use a direct trustee-to-trustee transfer (no QDRO needed).
Update beneficiaries on all retirement accounts after divorce — courts cannot override beneficiary designations.

terms.def: Qualified Domestic Relations Order — court order dividing retirement accounts without triggering the 10% early withdrawal penalty.
Funds that legally belong to you; unvested employer contributions may be forfeited.
The balance that accumulated during the marriage; pre-marital balances are separate property.

tasks.contacts: Divorce attorney (drafts the QDRO) • Plan administrator (HR or third-party) • Certified Financial Planner for tax advice
mistakes: Dividing a 401(k) without a QDRO — triggers a 30% tax and penalty hit.
Not updating beneficiaries after divorce — ex-spouse still receives proceeds.
Dividing Roth and Traditional IRAs equally without considering tax differences.
Assuming you can withdraw your ex-spouse's 401(k) and they'll roll it over.

tasks.title: Divorce and Business Ownership
tasks.what: How to value, protect, and divide a business owned by one or both spouses during divorce.
tasks.why: Business valuations are contested 40% of the time. A $500K undervaluation shifts your settlement by $250K in a 50/50 state. Without a valuation, you don't know what you're trading away.
steps: Determine the business structure (sole proprietorship, LLC, S-corp, C-corp) and ownership percentage.
Identify whether the business is marital or separate property (growth during marriage is marital).
Hire a business valuation expert ($3,000–$15,000) using income, asset, or market approach.
Gather 3–5 years of business tax returns.
Determine if the business will be sold, transferred to one spouse, or divided.
Document a post-divorce agreement with noncompete and confidentiality clauses.
Update all business documents and remove ex-spouse from signatory authority.

terms.def: Business value beyond tangible assets (brand reputation, customer loyalty); can increase valuation significantly.
Pre-existing agreement controlling what happens to ownership if a partner divorces or dies.

tasks.contacts: Business valuation appraiser (asa.org) • CPA or tax attorney • Divorce attorney for post-divorce agreements
mistakes: Using last year's tax return to value the business — doesn't account for trends.
Not accounting for owner's draw vs. retained earnings.
Keeping an ex-spouse on business loans or credit cards after divorce.

tasks.title: Divorce Tax Implications
tasks.what: Tax consequences of divorce: filing status changes, alimony deductibility, capital gains on the home, and QDRO treatment.
tasks.why: The 2018 Tax Cuts and Jobs Act eliminated alimony deductions for post-2018 decrees. Mishandling the home sale can trigger $75K+ in capital gains taxes. Dividing retirement accounts incorrectly can double your tax liability.
steps: Determine your filing status for the year of divorce (Single if divorced by Dec 31).
Decide who claims children as dependents — custodial parent gets the child tax credit ($2,000/child).
Understand post-2019 alimony: NOT deductible by payer, NOT taxable to receiver.
Value the primary residence and understand the $250K capital gains exclusion per spouse.
Document basis adjustments for all divided assets.
Update tax withholding on paychecks (use IRS W-4 calculator).
If non-custodial parent claims child, custodial parent must sign Form 8332.

terms.def: Spousal support paid after divorce. Post-2018 decrees: not deductible by payer, not taxable to receiver.
Profit on sale of an asset. Long-term gains taxed at 0%, 15%, or 20% depending on income.
Original cost of an asset used to calculate capital gains when sold.

tasks.contacts: Tax attorney or CPA specializing in divorce • IRS Publication 504 (Divorced or Separated Individuals) • AAML (aaml.org) for attorney referrals
mistakes: Treating alimony as deductible when the decree was finalized after 2018.
Underestimating capital gains taxes on the home.
Failing to request cost basis allocation when dividing investment accounts.
Not adjusting tax withholding to avoid a surprise tax bill.

tasks.title: Understand Your State's Divorce Process
tasks.what: The step-by-step legal process for dissolving your marriage, from filing to final decree.
tasks.why: Divorce varies dramatically by state. Uncontested divorces cost $500–$2,000 and take 2–4 months. Contested divorces average 18–24 months and $15,000–$50,000+ in legal fees.
steps: Identify your state's laws: waiting period, community vs. equitable distribution, residency requirement.
Gather required documents: marriage certificate, ID, SSN, financial documents.
Decide uncontested (agreed on all terms) or contested (disputed terms).
File the divorce petition at your county court clerk ($200–$500 filing fee).
Serve your spouse with divorce papers ($75–$300 for process server).
Spouse files a response within 20–30 days.
If contested: enter discovery phase (4–12 months of exchanging documents).
Attend mediation or settlement conference (60–70% of cases settle here).

terms.def: Both spouses agree on all terms; fast, cheap, minimal court involvement.
Spouses disagree on one or more terms; requires discovery, mediation, and possibly trial.
Divorce based on irreconcilable differences; all 50 states allow this.
Legal process of exchanging documents and information before trial.

tasks.contacts: State court website for divorce resources • State Bar Association for attorney referrals • Local legal aid office • DIY services (LawDepot, Nolo) for uncontested divorces
mistakes: Not meeting your state's residency requirement before filing.
Filing without the other spouse's Social Security number.
Not serving your spouse properly — invalid service means dismissal or delay.
Assuming no-fault divorce means you don't have to disclose finances.

script.intro: When calling a family law attorney for an initial consultation:
lines: "Hi, I'm looking for a family law attorney for a divorce. I'd like to schedule an initial consultation. Can you tell me your consultation fee, if any, and what I should bring to the first meeting?"

"Here's my situation: we've been married [X years], we [do/don't] have children, and we [do/don't] agree on the major terms. We [own/rent] our home and have [brief description of assets]. I'm trying to understand my options and what this process would look like."
"Can you give me a rough estimate of cost and timeline for my type of case? And do you handle mediation as well, or only litigation?"
script.note: Call 2–3 attorneys to compare. Many offer free 15–30 minute consultations. Bring your financial documents, marriage certificate, and a list of your questions. Don't sign a retainer at the first meeting — take time to decide.

tasks.title: Choose: Mediation vs. Collaborative vs. Litigation
tasks.what: Three paths to divorce with different costs, timelines, and outcomes.
tasks.why: Mediation costs $5K–$8K (2–4 months). Collaborative costs $25K–$50K (4–9 months). Litigation costs $78K–$200K+ (18–24+ months). Mediation couples are 40% more satisfied with outcomes.
steps: Mediation: hire a neutral mediator ($150–$300/hr); 10–20 hours total; each spouse pays 50%.
Collaborative: both attorneys agree in writing to withdraw if case goes to trial ($25K–$50K/side).
Litigation: full court case with discovery and trial ($78K–$200K+).
Choose mediation if amicable, no abuse, and simple assets.
Choose collaborative if you want attorney guidance in a non-adversarial process.
Choose litigation if spouse hides assets, there's abuse, or you can't reach agreement.
Interview mediators or attorneys before hiring — ask about experience, approach, and fees.
Get a fee agreement in writing specifying hourly rate, retainer, and billing frequency.

terms.def: Neutral third party facilitates negotiation; non-binding until both parties sign settlement.
Both attorneys agree to withdraw if case goes to trial; incentivizes settlement.
Upfront payment to an attorney that they bill against as they work.

tasks.contacts: Academy of Professional Mediators (mediate.org) • Collaborative Family Law Council (divorcecollaborative.org) • State Bar Association • Legal aid office
mistakes: Choosing mediation when there's been abuse — mediator can't enforce power balance.
Not interviewing attorneys before hiring — quality and cost vary widely.
Not getting a fee agreement in writing.
Assuming collaborative is faster than mediation — it's usually slower.

tasks.title: Custody and Parenting Plan
tasks.what: A written agreement specifying custody type, visitation schedule, decision-making authority, and communication protocols.
tasks.why: Courts prioritize "best interest of the child." Lack of a clear plan leads to conflict, inconsistency, and children caught in the middle. Custody modifications cost $3,000–$10,000.
steps: Understand custody types: sole legal, joint legal, joint legal and physical.
Determine the arrangement serving the child's best interest (age, health, school, parent relationships).
Draft a parenting schedule: weekday, weekend, holiday, and vacation schedules with specific times and locations.
Specify decision-making authority: medical, education, religious upbringing.
Include communication provisions: method, response time, emergency protocol.
Address conflict resolution: negotiation first, then mediator or family therapist.
Include provisions for schedule changes, makeup time, and documentation.

terms.def: The right to make major decisions about the child's upbringing (education, medical, religious).
The right to have the child live with you and make day-to-day decisions.
Modern term for visitation — the scheduled time a parent spends with the child.

tasks.contacts: Family law attorney ($200–$500/hr) • Court self-help center for free parenting plan templates • Custody mediator • Co-parenting therapist
mistakes: Creating a schedule that doesn't align with school/work calendars.
Not specifying holiday schedules — causes conflict every year.
Making child exchanges at home in high-conflict situations.
Assuming the non-custodial parent won't be involved — courts almost always order visitation.

tasks.title: High-Conflict Divorce and Parallel Parenting
tasks.what: Strategies for divorcing a high-conflict spouse while protecting yourself and limiting damage to children.
tasks.why: High-conflict divorce affects 30–40% of divorces. Children in high-conflict households have 2–3× higher rates of anxiety and depression. Parallel parenting and structured tools reduce conflict.
steps: Document everything: missed pickups, threatening messages, parental alienation, safety concerns.
Use a structured communication tool (OurFamilyWizard or Talking Parents — $100–$200/year) instead of direct contact.
Implement parallel parenting: each parent operates independently during their time; minimal communication.
Never badmouth the other parent, quiz children, or use them as messengers.
Request a parenting coordinator ($150–$300/hr) or guardian ad litem for severe conflict.
Document child safety concerns immediately and report to child protective services if needed.
File for a restraining order if there's abuse or threats (contact local domestic violence shelter).

terms.def: Each parent operates independently; minimal communication; only major decisions are shared.
One parent systematically turning the child against the other; courts may modify custody.
Court-appointed advocate investigating custody disputes and recommending the child's best interest.

tasks.contacts: National Domestic Violence Hotline: 1-800-799-7233 • Family law attorney • Parenting coordinator (afccnet.org) • Supervised visitation centers
mistakes: Communicating directly with a high-conflict ex instead of using a structured tool.
Badmouthing the ex to the child — harms the child and can lead to custody loss.
Failing to document abuse or threats — hard to prove later.
Using children as messengers or spies.

tasks.title: Determine Housing Options
tasks.what: A decision framework for the family home: who keeps it, refinancing, or selling and splitting proceeds.
tasks.why: The home is often the largest marital asset ($400K–$600K average). A spouse who keeps the home must qualify for a refinance alone. Selling too quickly misses appreciation; waiting too long racks up carrying costs.
steps: Get a professional appraisal ($300–$500) — don't rely on Zillow estimates.
Determine mortgage balance, interest rate, and monthly payment.
Calculate equity: Appraised Value − Mortgage Balance = equity to divide.
Evaluate Option A: one spouse keeps home and refinances (must qualify on own income, DTI ≤ 43%).
Evaluate Option B: sell the home and split proceeds (5–7% realtor commission, $250K capital gains exclusion per spouse).
Evaluate Option C: one moves out, sell later (stability for children, but ongoing conflict risk).
Protect the home during divorce: maintain insurance, keep paying mortgage, no unauthorized equity loans.

terms.def: Difference between home value and mortgage balance; marital property to be divided.
New mortgage to pay off the old one; allows one spouse to remove the other from the mortgage.
Total monthly debt ÷ gross income; lenders allow up to 43–50%.

tasks.contacts: Mortgage lender for pre-qualification • Real estate attorney • Real estate appraiser • Realtor (interview 2–3)
mistakes: Not getting an appraisal and using an old tax assessment (off by 20%+).
Keeping the home without running refinance numbers first.
Agreeing to "sell later" without specific terms — leads to disputes.
Not addressing who pays property tax and maintenance.

tasks.title: Renting After Divorce
tasks.what: Steps to find and secure rental housing when you may have lower income, damaged credit, or limited savings.
tasks.why: 40% of divorced people move within 12 months. Landlords screen harder post-divorce. A damaged credit score or limited income can block you from renting. Strategic moves now set you up for approval.
steps: Get your credit report (annualcreditreport.com) and dispute any errors.
Improve credit if below 600: pay bills on time, keep utilization under 30%, consider authorized user status.
Gather rental documents: photo ID, pay stubs, employment letter, bank statements, references.
Budget 30% of gross income for rent; landlords require income to be 3× the rent.
Apply strategically to 2–3 apartments at a time to avoid multiple hard credit inquiries.
Negotiate lease terms: month-to-month for flexibility, or 12-month for lowest rent.
Use a guarantor (co-signer) if needed — they must pass credit check too.
Build rental history with 12 months of on-time payments.

terms.def: Someone who signs the lease and guarantees rent payment if you can't pay.
Credit check that temporarily lowers your score by 5–10 points; recovers in 3–6 months.
Your record of on-time rent payments; good history makes future applications easier.

tasks.contacts: Landlord association in your area • Local legal aid for tenant rights • NFCC (nfcc.org) for free credit counseling
mistakes: Applying without gathered documents — landlord moves to next applicant.
Agreeing to unsustainable rent (over 40–50% of income).
Breaking the lease early — damages rental history for 2–3 years.
Not budgeting utilities separately from rent.

tasks.title: Notify Schools and Healthcare Providers
tasks.what: Communicating your new family structure to schools, doctors, and service providers so records are updated and both parents have appropriate access.
tasks.why: Without updated records, the wrong parent may be called in an emergency, or one parent may be denied access to school records and medical information.
steps: Notify school (principal and teacher) with updated custody order, contacts, and any access restrictions.
Complete school records release forms for both parents (unless custody order restricts).
Update insurance and emergency contacts at school.
Notify pediatrician and dentist with custody order and both parents' contact information.
Update health insurance coverage for children and provide new insurance card to school and doctor.
Notify therapy providers, extracurriculars, and sports organizations.
Keep copies of all updated forms in a file for reference.

terms.def: Federal law controlling school record access; schools can release to both parents unless custody order says otherwise.
Federal law controlling medical record access; doctors can release to both parents unless custody order restricts.
Parent with legal custody; automatically has access to school and medical records.

tasks.contacts: School office (principal, registrar) • Pediatrician's office • Your lawyer if access is refused
mistakes: Not providing school with a copy of the custody order.
Assuming both parents automatically have access to records.
Not updating insurance information — leads to billing errors.
Changing address but not notifying school and pediatrician.

tasks.title: Pet Custody
tasks.what: Determining who keeps the family pet and arranging care or split custody during and after divorce.
tasks.why: 67% of divorced couples dispute pet custody. In most states pets are property, but some states (California, Illinois, Alaska) now consider the animal's best interest. Pet care costs $1,500–$3,000/year; emergencies can cost $5,000–$15,000.
steps: Check your state's pet custody law (search "[state] pet custody law").
Determine who should keep the pet: primary caregiver, stable housing, time/energy, child's bond.
Negotiate pet ownership in the divorce settlement: custody, visitation, vet costs, medical decisions.
If you can't agree, request a pet custody arrangement: primary, alternating, or split custody.
Create a pet agreement: vet authorization, microchip info, vaccination schedule, cost split.
Transfer microchip registration, vet records, and pet insurance to the owner.

terms.def: Both spouses retain ownership and share custody; rare, requires high cooperation.
Pet ID chip implanted under the skin; contains owner contact info and can be scanned at any vet.

tasks.contacts: Divorce attorney for settlement language • Veterinarian for care advice • State bar for pet custody law info
mistakes: Not addressing pets in the divorce settlement — leads to disputes.
Not updating microchip and vet records — blocks emergency access.
Joint custody without a specific visitation schedule.
Assuming one spouse automatically gets the pet.

tasks.title: Supporting Children Through Divorce
tasks.what: Age-appropriate ways to talk to children about divorce, recognize distress, and access mental health support.
tasks.why: Children in divorces have 2–3× higher rates of anxiety and depression, but with proper support, 80% adjust well within 2 years. Untreated childhood anxiety can lead to long-term academic and emotional problems.
steps: Talk to children together (both parents) if possible — use simple, honest language; reassure it's not their fault.
Adjust by age: 2–5 (very simple), 6–11 (explain schedule and reassure about school/friends), 12+ (acknowledge difficulty, ask their concerns).
Recognize signs of distress: acting out, grade drops, withdrawal, sleep/appetite changes.
Enroll children in therapy: play therapy (ages 3–7) or CBT (ages 8+); $75–$150/hour.
Maintain stability and routine: same school, consistent custody schedule, both parents involved.
Never put children in the middle — no badmouthing, no using them as messengers or spies.
Monitor for serious issues: suicidal thoughts, self-harm, substance use — call pediatrician immediately.
Access support groups: DivorceCare for Kids, school counselors, NAMI (1-800-950-NAMI).

terms.def: Therapeutic approach using play and art to help young children process emotions.
Talk therapy teaching children to recognize thought patterns and develop coping strategies.

tasks.contacts: Child therapist (Psychology Today's finder) • Pediatrician • School counselor (free) • DivorceCare for Kids (divorcecare.org) • NAMI: 1-800-950-NAMI
mistakes: Not talking to children about the divorce — they find out from others.
Over-explaining or sharing adult conflict details.
Assuming children will bounce back without support.
Not maintaining routine and consistency.

tasks.title: Co-Parenting Communication Protocols
tasks.what: Structured communication tools and agreements to coordinate parenting, share information, and minimize conflict.
tasks.why: Poor communication confuses children and creates logistical chaos. Structured tools reduce conflict by 30–50% and improve compliance with custody orders.
steps: Choose a co-parenting app (OurFamilyWizard or Talking Parents; $100–$200/year) — all messages timestamped, archived, and court-admissible.
Create a communication agreement: method, 24-hour response time, emergency protocol, professional tone.
Set up a shared calendar with custody schedule, school events, medical appointments, holidays.
Track expenses in the app and agree on a cost-split with monthly settlement.
Create a custody exchange protocol: neutral location, exact time, 5-minute max, no lingering.
Create a holiday and vacation schedule with 30-day advance notice requirement.
Specify decision-making authority: medical (routine = either parent; major = both agree), education, activities.

terms.def: Dedicated co-parenting platform (OurFamilyWizard, Talking Parents); timestamped, archived, court-admissible.
Agreed process for transferring child between parents: time, location, what's exchanged.
Time owed to a parent if the other parent misses their scheduled custody time.

tasks.contacts: Co-parenting app support • Family law attorney to draft communication agreement • Mediator or family therapist
mistakes: Using personal text/email instead of a dedicated app.
Not specifying exchange location and time — leads to conflict every handoff.
Not tracking expenses — one parent resents unpaid reimbursements.
Changing the schedule informally without documenting it.

tasks.title: Military Pension Division — USFSPA and 10/10 Rule
tasks.what: How military pensions are divided in divorce and why the 10/10 rule determines whether ex-spouses receive direct payments.
tasks.why: Military pensions average $20,000–$60,000/year. If the couple was married 10+ years during military service, the ex-spouse gets direct automatic payments from DFAS. Under 10 years, the service member must pay personally (often doesn't happen).
steps: Verify USFSPA eligibility: married 10+ years, with 10+ years overlapping active military service.
If not 10/10 eligible: negotiate other assets of equal value or a QDRO-like court order.
Calculate pension division: (years married during service ÷ total service years) × 50%.
File for direct payment with DFAS after divorce is final (60–90 days processing).
Address Survivor Benefit Plan (SBP) in settlement — guarantees payments continue if service member dies.
Update beneficiary designations on the pension.
Track pension payments once they begin and verify DFAS calculation.

terms.def: Uniformed Services Former Spouses' Protection Act — federal law governing military pension division.
If married 10+ years during military service, ex-spouse qualifies for direct pension payments from DFAS.
Optional military insurance guaranteeing pension payments continue to beneficiary after service member's death.
Defense Finance and Accounting Service — pays military pensions and handles division.

tasks.contacts: Military Family Law attorney (critical) • DFAS: 1-800-321-1080 or dfas.mil • Veterans Service Officer (VFW, American Legion)
mistakes: Not realizing the 10/10 rule applies and settling for less.
Not addressing SBP in settlement — leaves ex-spouse at risk.
Accepting less than 50% without understanding the direct payment benefit.
Assuming ex-spouse can't get pension if marriage was less than 10 years.

tasks.title: TRICARE Eligibility for Ex-Spouses — 20/20/20 Rule
tasks.what: The 20/20/20 rule determines whether an ex-spouse keeps TRICARE (military health insurance) after divorce.
tasks.why: TRICARE costs $200–$500/year vs. $10,000–$15,000 for civilian insurance. The 20/20/20 rule is the difference between affordable coverage and financial hardship.
steps: Check 20/20/20 eligibility: married 20+ years, with 20+ years overlapping military service.
If not eligible: ex-spouse gets 36-month TRICARE continuation (like COBRA).
Apply for TRICARE at milconnect.dmdc.osd.mil if eligible.
Plan for insurance alternatives after the 36-month continuation: marketplace, employer plan, Medicaid.
Update TRICARE for children — they stay on TRICARE even after divorce.
Address TRICARE explicitly in the divorce settlement.

terms.def: Ex-spouse keeps TRICARE permanently if married 20+ years during 20+ years of military service.
36-month continuation after divorce for ineligible ex-spouses; similar to civilian COBRA.

tasks.contacts: TRICARE: 1-800-333-8387 or tricare.mil • Military Family Law attorney • Veterans Service Officer
mistakes: Not realizing 20/20/20 applies and planning for loss of TRICARE.
Assuming children automatically keep TRICARE — custodial parent must be listed correctly.
Not addressing TRICARE in the settlement.

tasks.title: PCS and Deployment Complications During Divorce
tasks.what: How PCS (Permanent Change of Station) and deployment affect divorce proceedings, custody, and service.
tasks.why: A service member can be PCS'd mid-divorce to another country, pausing proceedings. Deployment can delay divorce by months. Understanding military service rules prevents surprises.
steps: Understand PCS timing and its impact on custody (child may not be able to follow).
If PCS'd: address it in custody plan — child remains with custodial parent or relocates.
Understand deployment holds — some states pause divorce during deployment.
Set up military allotment for automatic child support during deployment.
Address military service obligations and PCS notification requirements in the settlement.
Plan for post-deployment or post-retirement custody changes.
Get military power of attorney from JAG if needed for medical/educational decisions.

terms.def: Permanent Change of Station — military reassignment to a new duty location.
Temporary absence for military operations; typically 4–12 months.
Automatic deduction from military pay for child support or spousal support.
Judge Advocate General — military legal services offering free advice to service members and family.

tasks.contacts: Service member's JAG office (free legal advice) • Military Family Law attorney • State Bar for military divorce experience
mistakes: Not realizing the service member can be PCS'd mid-divorce.
Assuming military allotment for child support happens automatically — it must be set up.
Not planning for post-retirement custody changes.
Serving divorce papers without accounting for deployment.

tasks.title: Financial Planning for Stay-at-Home Parents
tasks.what: Strategies for lower-earning or stay-at-home spouses to rebuild financial independence post-divorce.
tasks.why: Stay-at-home parents lose 10+ years of earning history and retirement savings. Spousal support (20–50% of income gap for 5–10 years) and Social Security ex-spouse benefits (up to 50% of ex's benefit at age 62+) are critical bridges to independence.
steps: Understand spousal support types: temporary, rehabilitative (time-limited), indefinite, or reimbursement.
Negotiate support amount based on marriage length, income gap, and standard of living.
Plan workforce re-entry: assess skills, identify gaps, explore training ($5K–$25K covered by support).
Build employment history during rehabilitation period — start part-time and increase gradually.
Protect support payments in settlement: specify duration, termination events, enforcement mechanisms.
Plan for Social Security ex-spouse benefits at age 62+ (up to 50% of ex's PIA if married 10+ years).
Start building your own retirement savings: contribute to 401(k) or IRA as soon as possible.

terms.def: Payments from higher-earning spouse post-divorce; rehabilitative (time-limited) or indefinite.
Social Security benefit based on ex-spouse's work history; up to 50% of their PIA at age 62+.
Baseline Social Security benefit calculated from 35 highest-earning years.

tasks.contacts: Divorce attorney • Social Security: 1-800-772-1213 • Career counselor or workforce center • Certified Financial Planner for retirement planning
mistakes: Not negotiating spousal support and relying on voluntary help.
Failing to plan for workforce re-entry and skills updates.
Not realizing Social Security ex-spouse benefits exist — $200K–$400K in lifetime benefits.
Accepting a lump sum when ongoing support is more secure.

tasks.title: Name Changes and Document Updates
tasks.what: The legal process of changing your name after divorce and updating all identification, financial, and legal documents.
tasks.why: Name changes take 2–3 months and require updates to dozens of documents. Delays cause credit report issues, rejected tax filings, and passport renewal problems.
steps: Petition for name change through your divorce (included in filing fee) or file separately ($150–$500).
Update state driver's license/ID at DMV with divorce decree ($20–$50).
Update passport with US State Department ($130; 2–3 months processing).
Update Social Security card (free; 1–2 weeks) — do this before other updates.
Update all financial accounts: banks, credit cards, investments, retirement, insurance.
Update employment and tax records: HR, IRS (Form SS-5), state tax office.
Update property and legal documents: deed, vehicle titles, wills, trusts, POA.

terms.def: Your surname before marriage; often the name returned to after divorce.
Formal order from a judge authorizing your legal name change.

tasks.contacts: County court clerk for name change petition • DMV for license and title updates • SSA: 1-800-772-1213 • Bank, credit card, insurance companies
mistakes: Not updating Social Security first — all other name changes flow from your SSN.
Not filing a deed correction — home title remains in married name.
Updating financial accounts but not the IRS — causes tax return rejections.
Assuming name change happens automatically through divorce.

tasks.title: Post-Divorce Financial Reset Checklist
tasks.what: A comprehensive checklist to reorganize your financial and legal life post-divorce.
tasks.why: An ex-spouse left on a will, life insurance beneficiary, or healthcare POA creates major problems. This checklist takes 4–6 hours but prevents years of unwanted entanglement.
steps: Update all beneficiary designations: life insurance, retirement accounts, bank accounts, brokerage accounts.
Update your will (remove ex-spouse as executor/beneficiary; name guardian for minor children).
Update power of attorney and healthcare proxy — remove ex-spouse.
Review and rebalance retirement accounts; build a 3–6 month emergency fund.
Pull credit reports, dispute errors, and monitor monthly for 6–12 months.
Update insurance: health, auto, homeowners/renters, life, disability.
Update tax withholding to Single or Head of Household; use IRS W-4 calculator.
Update all records: driver's license, voter registration, passport, professional licenses, emergency contacts.
Audit digital accounts: change passwords, update recovery info, separate shared cloud storage.

terms.def: Bank account that transfers to a named beneficiary at death, bypassing probate.
Person authorized to make medical decisions if you're incapacitated.
Person who manages and distributes your estate after death (named in your will).

tasks.contacts: Estate planning attorney ($500–$2,000) • CPA for tax withholding • Financial advisor • Insurance broker (free service)
mistakes: Not updating beneficiaries — ex-spouse receives proceeds if they outlive you.
Not creating or updating a will — state decides who gets your assets.
Forgetting to update POA — ex-spouse still has authority over decisions.
Not adjusting tax withholding — surprise tax bill next year.
